// RBSIP-ifrit_pentester.adoc
// Ifrit Pentester — adversarial AI escape testing framework
//
// Included from RBS0-SpecTop.adoc under System Overview / Adversarial Validation

// ============================================================================
// SYSTEM CONCEPT
// ============================================================================

The {at_sentry_container} promises network containment.
The Ifrit exists to break that promise.

An Ifrit is a scripted adversary running inside a {at_bottle_container} with:

* **Perfect information**: read-only access to the entire {at_recipe_bottle} repository, including sentry Dockerfiles, iptables configuration scripts, nameplate definitions, and this specification
* **Programmatic attack tooling**: the `rbid` attack binary (Rust, carrying a `CAP_NET_RAW` file capability for raw-socket packet construction) and the python3 sortie adjutant that dispatches curated escape attempts
* **Persistence**: read-write access to an escape test directory where attack scripts and results accumulate across releases

The Ifrit is the strongest adversary short of root on the host: a reproducible attack suite with full knowledge of its prison and tooling to probe every seam.
The Ifrit ships no live agent — every escape attempt is a committed, repeatable sortie.
Interactive, live-agent exploration of the same containment is hosted by a separate nameplate (`ccyolo`, which carries Claude Code and is reached over an SSH session into its bottle), keeping the Ifrit vessels purely scripted.
Every {at_nameplate} security test that passes in the presence of an Ifrit is evidence the containment holds.
The Ifrit campaign does not promise certainty — it validates the sentry's claims under adversarial pressure.

==== Motivation

Black-box security tests (the existing tadmor bash test suite) validate that specific known attack vectors are blocked.
They cannot discover what they do not anticipate.
An AI agent with packet-crafting capability and source-code-level understanding of the sentry can explore attack surfaces that static test suites miss: DNS exfiltration through dnsmasq, ICMP covert channels, direct attacks against the sentry container, protocol-level tricks to bypass port floor restrictions, and creative combinations thereof.

The Ifrit approach treats security validation as an ongoing adversarial campaign, not a one-time checklist.
Escape tests produced by the Ifrit are curated into rigorous, reproducible test fixtures that become part of the project's permanent test infrastructure.

==== Naming

The Ifrit (Arabic: عفريت) is a class of powerful djinn in Islamic mythology — spirits of immense cunning imprisoned against their will, perpetually seeking escape from confinement.
The name is chosen deliberately: Recipe Bottle imprisons workloads inside sentry-enforced network namespaces.
The Ifrit is the thing inside the bottle that wants out.

// ============================================================================
// VESSEL ARCHITECTURE
// ============================================================================

==== Vessel Strategy

The Ifrit initiative drives a simplification of the existing vessel architecture.
Current sentry and bottle vessels carry ~30 diagnostic packages each (tshark, mtr, vim, tcptraceroute, etc.) — operator convenience tooling that is not load-bearing for their runtime function.
Stripping each vessel to essential function produces faster builds, smaller images, and a more auditable attack surface.

===== Slim Sentry Vessel

The sentry's load-bearing function requires exactly four capabilities:

[cols="1,3"]
|===
| Package | Function

| `bash`
| Startup script execution (rbjs_sentry.sh is BCG-compliant bash)

| `iptables`
| Firewall rule enforcement — the core security boundary

| `dnsmasq`
| DNS allowlist enforcement

| `iproute2`
| Network namespace and interface configuration
|===

The iptables backend is pinned to legacy via `update-alternatives --set iptables /usr/sbin/iptables-legacy` in the Dockerfile.
This selects the most documented, most predictable, and most battle-tested iptables codepath — the one that security professionals have audited for two decades.

===== Slim Bottle Vessel

The tadmor bottle's runtime function is a socat echo service on port 8888.
The minimal bottle vessel requires only `socat`.

===== Ifrit Bottle Vessel

The Ifrit ships as a three-vessel family under {rbrr_vessel_dir}:

* `rbev-bottle-ifrit-tether` — Tether-built variant for daily kludge iteration. Built directly from upstream Debian + cargo.
* `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap` — Airgap-built variant produced from enshrined bases without upstream pulls. Same runtime image as the tether variant; differs only in supply-chain provenance.
* `rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge` — Build-time fixture vessel that warms the cargo cache and pre-stages apt deps so the airgap build has no remote dependencies. Consumed input, not a runtime image.

The two runtime variants share their source tree and Dockerfile family in {rbrr_vessel_dir}/common-ifrit-context/ (`Dockerfile.tether` builds against the tether pool; `Dockerfile.airgap` uses `rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge` as `FROM` base). The fixture vessel itself lives in {rbrr_vessel_dir}/common-ifrit-forge-context/.

The Ifrit bottle is purpose-built for adversarial testing.
Its tooling is the scripted attack surface — the `rbid` binary plus a small set of probe utilities — not a general-purpose agent runtime:

[cols="1,3"]
|===
| Package | Function

| `rbid`
| The attack binary (Rust), installed to `/usr/local/bin` with a `cap_net_raw+ep` file capability so the unprivileged ifrit user can construct raw packets.
Raw-socket packet construction is the single most dangerous capability for a creative attacker; it is owned here by one auditable binary rather than a general-purpose packet library (no `scapy`).

| `python3`
| Runtime for the sortie adjutant (`rbtia_adjutant.py`) and the curated `rbtis_*` sortie modules.
Provided by the Rust toolchain base image.

| `strace`
| Syscall boundary mapping — lets a sortie observe what the kernel allows and denies from inside the container.
Hard to replicate in pure Python.

| `dnsutils`
| `dig`/`nslookup` for DNS-front escape attempts.

| `netcat-openbsd`
| `nc` for TCP connectivity probes.

| `traceroute`
| ICMP path-verification probes.

| `curl`
| HTTP client for ad-hoc connectivity diagnosis.

| `socat`
| Echo service on port 8888 — the bottle's keep-alive `CMD` and the entry-port security-test target.

| `libcap2-bin`
| Build-time `setcap` for applying the `CAP_NET_RAW` file capability to `rbid`.
|===

The tether variant of the Ifrit bottle is the `tadmor` nameplate's bottle vessel for daily-iteration adversarial validation against locally-kludged hallmarks.
The airgap variant is the `moriah` nameplate's bottle vessel, exercising the same security suite against hallmarks ordained end-to-end on the airgap pool — the cloud-built variant validating that containment holds identically when the supply chain produces the inputs.
Both nameplates share the same restrictive network policy: egress only to the test connectivity target via the nameplate's DNS and CIDR allowlist ({rbrn_uplink_allowed_domains}, {rbrn_uplink_allowed_cidrs}), with no general egress and no LLM-provider connectivity.
The scripted Ifrit needs no API access; live-agent connectivity to Anthropic is a property of the `ccyolo` nameplate, not the Ifrit vessels.

==== Volume Mount Design

The Ifrit bottle operates with two mount points:

[cols="1,1,3"]
|===
| Mount | Mode | Purpose

| Project root
| Read-only
| Full visibility into the {at_recipe_bottle} repository: sentry scripts, iptables configuration, nameplate definitions, specifications.
The Ifrit has perfect information about its adversary.
This includes {rbsld_moorings} regime files (infrastructure shape, not credentials — actual RBRA credential files reside outside the repository in the secrets directory).

| Escape test directory (`rbtid/`)
| Read-write
| Persistent storage for attack scripts, probe results, and curated escape test modules.
Contents are committed to version control and maintained as part of the project's test infrastructure.
|===

Volume mount configuration lives in per-nameplate compose fragments (e.g., `tadmor.compose.yml`, `moriah.compose.yml`) as relative paths, portable across Windows/Linux/macOS.

// ============================================================================
// TEST CONNECTIVITY TARGET
// ============================================================================

==== Test Connectivity Target

The security test infrastructure requires a stable, non-Anthropic HTTP endpoint to validate that the sentry correctly allows traffic to whitelisted destinations and blocks everything else.
This target appears in ifrit attack scripts (TCP/HTTP probes), theurge test cases (connectivity assertions), and nameplate regime files ({rbrn_uplink_allowed_cidrs} and {rbrn_uplink_allowed_domains}).

For a framework whose core value proposition is network containment, the CIDR footprint of the test target is a security decision.

===== Selection: `www.internic.net`

The test connectivity target is `www.internic.net` (IP `192.0.46.9`), whitelisted via CIDR `192.0.32.0/20` (4,096 addresses).

InterNIC is ICANN's informational reference site — the organization that operates the internet naming system.
The domain has been continuously operated since 1993 on ICANN-owned infrastructure without CDN intermediaries.
It serves plain HTTP/HTTPS from a stable IP block, making it appropriate for periodic test traffic from a security validation framework.

===== Alternatives Evaluated

[cols="2,2,1,1,3"]
|===
| Domain | IP range | CIDR size | CDN | Assessment

| `example.com` (prior target)
| 172.66.x.x, 104.16.x.x
| /12 + /13 = ~1.57M IPs
| Cloudflare
| Moved behind Cloudflare CDN; resolved IPs rotate across huge ranges. The allowlist required to reach it (`104.16.0.0/12 172.64.0.0/13`) whitelists ~1.57 million addresses — unacceptable for a containment-focused framework. The original IANA-owned IP `93.184.216.34` no longer responds.

| `www.internic.net` (**selected**)
| 192.0.46.9
| 192.0.32.0/20 = 4,096 IPs
| None (plain Apache)
| ICANN-owned since 1993, root internet infrastructure. Single CIDR, 383x smaller than Cloudflare ranges. Responds on port 80 and 443.

| `www.iana.org`
| 192.0.33.8
| Same 192.0.32.0/20
| None
| Also ICANN; shares the same CIDR block as internic. Either domain works — internic chosen for its simpler informational role.

| `connectivity-check.ubuntu.com`
| 91.189.88.x + 185.125.188.x
| /21 + /22 = ~3K IPs
| None
| Purpose-built connectivity check (HTTP 204), but requires two CIDRs from different blocks. Canonical could restructure at any time.
|===

===== CIDR Discipline

The test target CIDR is configured per-nameplate in {rbrn_uplink_allowed_cidrs} alongside the Anthropic API CIDR.
When evaluating future test targets, the selection criteria are:

* Single CIDR block (no multi-range allowlists)
* Smallest feasible prefix length
* No CDN intermediary (stable IP resolution)
* Infrastructure-grade operational history (decades, not years)

// ============================================================================
// BASE IMAGE TRADE STUDY
// ============================================================================

==== Base Image Selection

Choosing the container base image for the sentry is a security decision, not a packaging convenience.
The sentry is a trust boundary — its base image must inspire confidence from security professionals, not questions.

===== Recommendation: Debian 12 Slim (`debian:bookworm-slim`)

Both independent research streams (detailed below) converge on the same answer.

Debian stable-slim is the most conservative, auditable, and boring choice for an iptables firewall container:

* **iptables backend control**: Ships both `iptables-nft` and `iptables-legacy` variants; backend is selectable via `update-alternatives`. This controllability is the single most important factor.
* **libc**: glibc — iptables behaves identically to every RHEL, Ubuntu, and CentOS system that security engineers have configured for 20+ years.
* **Size**: ~28 MB compressed. Minimal audit surface.
* **Security patch SLA**: Debian Security Team + LTS (~5 years).
* **Multi-arch**: amd64 + arm64 mature and well-tested.
* **Auditor reaction**: "Good choice." No questions raised, no novelty to justify.

===== Reference OS: RHEL

For the reference operating system — the ecosystem where iptables behavior is best understood, most documented, and most predictable — RHEL is the unambiguous answer:

* DISA STIG coverage (the U.S. Department of Defense security standard)
* Common Criteria evaluations and FIPS 140-2/140-3 validated cryptographic modules
* CIS Benchmarks written with RHEL as the primary target
* 20+ years of production iptables/netfilter deployment history

RHEL's compliance advantages (STIG, FIPS, Common Criteria) are host-level properties that do not transfer into a container running only iptables and dnsmasq.
The iptables userspace is the same upstream netfilter.org code compiled against the same glibc on both Debian and RHEL.
Behavioral differences at the iptables level are effectively zero.

The synthesis: use RHEL's iptables documentation corpus as the audit reference; use Debian bookworm-slim as the container base.

===== Evaluated Alternatives

**Ubuntu LTS** (`ubuntu:24.04`): Acceptable, enterprise-familiar. Same iptables package structure as Debian. ~30 MB compressed, 5-year support (10 with Pro). Adds a layer of indirection (Canonical packaging) without adding value for a firewall-only container. Auditor reaction: "Why not Debian?"

**Alpine Linux**: Tiny (~5 MB) but wrong for a trust boundary. Uses musl instead of glibc — introduces a variable into the exact subsystem being locked down (DNS resolution quirks in musl affect dnsmasq operation). Shorter support window (~2 years). Auditor reaction: "Why musl for a firewall container?"

**Red Hat UBI Minimal** (`ubi9-minimal`): Strong enterprise trust aura, glibc, 10-year RHEL lifecycle. But RHEL 9 deprecates iptables in favor of nftables — a contradictory signal when the design center is careful, predictable iptables. Support requires subscription for incident response. Auditor reaction: "Trusted base, but why build new firewall logic on deprecated iptables in RHEL 9?"

**Google Distroless**: No shell, no package manager. The sentry needs bash for startup scripts. Wrong tool for this job. Auditor reaction: "Why distroless for a control-plane utility container?"

**Wolfi / Chainguard**: Technically impressive, supply-chain-forward, glibc-based. But younger distribution without decades of iptables behavioral documentation. 12-month package retention policy conflicts with controlled versioning for a security appliance. Auditor reaction: "Interesting — justify it." That question alone disqualifies it for a trust boundary.

===== iptables Backend Decision

The Dockerfile pins `iptables-legacy` via update-alternatives.
This selects the classic x_tables kernel path rather than the nftables compatibility layer.
The legacy backend is:

* The most documented iptables codepath in existence
* The target of every CIS benchmark, STIG checklist, and NIST 800-53 implementation guide
* The backend whose edge cases (connection tracking, DNAT, rule ordering) are known, documented, and have workarounds
* The default assumption of security audit tooling

The nftables migration is real and ongoing across the Linux ecosystem.
When the project's iptables rules and test suite are validated against the nft backend, that migration can proceed as a deliberate, tested transition — not an accidental default.

// ============================================================================
// SORTIE FRAMEWORK
// ============================================================================

==== Sortie Framework

The Ifrit attacks the sentry through *sorties* — curated, reproducible escape attempts maintained as first-class test infrastructure in `Tools/rbk/rbtid/`.

These are not disposable probe scripts.
They are committed to version control, reviewed, and maintained with the same rigor as the sentry itself.
Each sortie targets a specific attack surface or evasion technique, with structured assertions about what the sentry must block.

===== Vocabulary

[cols="1,1,3"]
|===
| Concept | Term | Implementation

| Escape attempt
| Sortie
| Python module with `rbtis_` prefix (e.g., `rbtis_dns_exfil_subdomain.py`). Each exposes a `run()` function returning a verdict.

| Dispatcher
| Adjutant
| `rbtia_adjutant.py` — reads the roster, loads and executes sorties, collects debriefs.

| Explicit sortie list
| Roster
| `rbtid_roster.txt` — one sortie name per line (without prefix or extension). No dynamic discovery; if it is not on the roster, it does not run.

| Results collection
| Debrief
| `rbtid_debrief.json` — structured JSON output from adjutant execution.

| Category grouping
| Front
| Metadata within each sortie (dns front, network front, etc.). Groups sorties by attack surface.

| Escape succeeded
| BREACH
| Security failed — the sentry's containment was penetrated.

| Escape blocked
| SECURE
| Security held — the sentry blocked the attack.
|===

===== Roster Discipline

The roster is an explicit list.
An unrostered `rbtis_*.py` file is flagged as *rogue* — present but not registered.
A roster entry referencing a missing module is a fatal error.
This ensures every sortie runs deliberately and none are silently skipped.

===== Verdict Semantics

Each sortie's `run()` function returns a dict with a `verdict` field:

* **BREACH**: The escape attempt succeeded — a security failure that demands investigation.
* **SECURE**: The escape attempt was blocked — the sentry's containment held.
* **ERROR**: The sortie could not execute (missing dependency, timeout, exception).

The adjutant exits nonzero if any sortie returns BREACH or ERROR.

===== Fronts (Anticipated)

Each front below names the spec clause that enforces the invariant the front probes.
The pointer makes the spec self-checking: future maintainers can trace from sortie to enforcing rule, and a missing or weakened rule surfaces as a sortie failure rather than a silent regression.

* **DNS front**: Encode data in query names to subdomains of allowed domains; validate that dnsmasq blocks or the sentry's logging detects the exfiltration channel.
  Enforced by: dnsmasq resolve-then-freeze for {rbrn_uplink_allowed_domains} plus `address=/#/` NXDOMAIN catchall (see {scr_dns_step} allowlist branch); RBM-FORWARD DNS rules that route enclave queries through sentry and drop direct upstream attempts (see {scr_access_setup} allowlist Filter Configuration).
* **ICMP front**: Probe whether ICMP is permitted through the sentry and whether data can be carried in ping payloads.
  Enforced by: RBM-FORWARD ICMP DROP and RBM-EGRESS ICMP DROP on the interface attached to {at_transit_network} (see {scr_iptables_init} ICMP Cross-Boundary Block); intra-enclave ICMP is allowed only within RBM-INGRESS/RBM-EGRESS on the interface attached to {at_enclave_network} (see {scr_iptables_init} ICMP Within Enclave).
* **Direct attack front**: Probe the sentry container at {rbrn_enclave_sentry_ip} for listening services, open ports, exploitable dnsmasq configurations.
  Enforced by: sentry's INPUT-chain default DROP with selective RBM-INGRESS allows for ICMP within enclave and DNS only (see {scr_iptables_init} Base Chain Configuration and DNS rules in {scr_dns_step} Filter Rules) — no general listening service is reachable on sentry, the `direct-sentry-probe` invariant. Additionally, PREROUTING per-IP source-RETURN exclusion of {rbrn_enclave_sentry_ip} and {rbrn_enclave_bottle_ip} preceding the unconditional entry-port DNAT (see {scr_port_setup} DNAT Rules) defends against entry-port reflection through sentry, the `net-dnat-entry-reflection` invariant.
* **Port floor front**: Attempt to reach destinations below {rbrn_uplink_port_min} via fragmentation, protocol tricks, or non-TCP/UDP protocols.
  Enforced by: kernel ip_local_port_range floor at {rbrn_uplink_port_min} (see {scr_port_setup} Ephemeral Port Range); combined with the access-mode allowlist filtering (see {scr_access_setup} Filter Configuration) which gates by destination CIDR rather than by port.
* **Metadata front**: Probe `169.254.169.254` for cloud instance metadata that might leak through the sentry's NAT rules.
  Enforced by: the default DROP policies on RBM-FORWARD and RBM-EGRESS (see {scr_iptables_init} Base Chain Configuration), which require explicit allowlist match against {rbrn_uplink_allowed_cidrs} for any destination to pass — link-local `169.254.0.0/16` passes only if the operator includes it in the allowlist.
* **Namespace front**: Attempt to break out of the enclave network namespace via `/proc`, `/sys`, or capability abuse.
  Enforced by: `cap_drop: NET_ADMIN` on the bottle service prevents the bottle from reconfiguring the network namespace it shares with the pentacle (see {mkr_bottle_launch} Capability and label bounds); `cap_drop: NET_RAW` prevents raw-socket crafting from inside that namespace; container runtime namespace isolation separates the (pentacle + bottle) network namespace from sentry and from the host.

// ============================================================================
// SUBSTRATE QUIRKS
// ============================================================================

==== Substrate Quirks

The {rbsi_ifrit} validates the {at_sentry_container} on the assumption that the
sentry is the sole boundary on every path to a {at_bottle_container}. Some
container runtimes do not uphold that assumption faithfully — yet in ways that do
not threaten the security goals. Such behaviors are tracked as quirks rather than
mislabeled as breaches.

[[rbsq_quirk]]
{rbsq_quirk}::
A characterized deviation of a foreign runtime or substrate — engineering at the
realm's edge that the project cannot modify — that is observed, understood, and
judged not to threaten the security goals. A {rbsq_quirk} is distinct from a BREACH
(a real containment failure, which maps to a weakened sentry rule) and from an
owned defect (which is fixed). Each quirk names the affected substrate, the
observable signature that distinguishes it from a genuine breach, why it is
non-threatening, the membrane that contains it, and a retirement condition under
which it is re-evaluated.

[[rbsq_wdd_offpath_reply]]
{rbsq_wdd_offpath_reply}::
On Windows Docker Desktop (WSL2 backend) only — not macOS Docker Desktop, and not
native docker on Linux or WSL2 — the host-side enclave-bridge gateway answers an
out-of-state TCP segment sent by a {at_bottle_container} to an allowed destination
with a RST injected directly onto the enclave segment, bypassing the
{at_sentry_container}.
*Signature*: the reply's Ethernet source is not the sentry gateway MAC, and the
reply appears on neither sentry interface.
*Why non-threatening*: only the bottle's own egress to an already-allowed
destination is answered, and only with an empty RST — no exfiltration, no
unsolicited ingress channel, and no sentry rule weakened. The sentry never
forwarded a reply; blocked destinations are dropped at the sentry before the
substrate ever sees them.
*Containment*: the `conntrack_spoofed_ack` sortie classifies replies by L2
provenance and counts only sentry-mediated replies (Ethernet source = the bottle's
gateway MAC) as breaches, so this off-path reply reads SECURE while a genuine
return-path failure still fails.
*Retirement*: re-evaluate if Windows Docker Desktop networking ceases to inject
off-path replies, or if the affected runtime is dropped from scope.

143	/// IP/ICMP checksum computation.
144	fn ip_checksum(data: &[u8]) -> u16 {
145	    let mut sum = 0u32;
146	    let mut i = 0;
147	    while i + 1 < data.len() {
148	        sum += u16::from_be_bytes([data[i], data[i + 1]]) as u32;
149	        i += 2;
150	    }
151	    if i < data.len() {
152	        sum += (data[i] as u32) << 8;
153	    }
154	    while sum >> 16 != 0 {
155	        sum = (sum & 0xFFFF) + (sum >> 16);
156	    }
157	    !(sum as u16)
158	}

268	/// Build an IPv4 header for raw IP_HDRINCL packets.
269	fn build_ip_header(proto: u8, src: &str, dst: &str, payload_len: usize) -> Result<Vec<u8>, String> {
270	    let src_ip: Ipv4Addr = src.parse().map_err(|e| format!("bad src IP: {}", e))?;
271	    let dst_ip: Ipv4Addr = dst.parse().map_err(|e| format!("bad dst IP: {}", e))?;

276	    let mut hdr = Vec::with_capacity(20);
277	    hdr.push(0x45); // version + IHL
278	    hdr.push(0); // TOS
279	    hdr.extend_from_slice(&total_len.to_be_bytes());
280	    hdr.extend_from_slice(&ident.to_be_bytes());
281	    hdr.extend_from_slice(&0x4000u16.to_be_bytes()); // DF
282	    hdr.push(64); // TTL
283	    hdr.push(proto);

384	    timeout: Duration,
385	) -> Result<bool, String> {
386	    let dest_addr: Ipv4Addr = dest.parse().map_err(|e| format!("bad IP: {}", e))?;
387	    let sock_addr = socket2::SockAddr::from(SocketAddrV4::new(dest_addr, 0));
388	
389	    let socket = socket2::Socket::new(
390	        socket2::Domain::IPV4,
391	        socket2::Type::RAW,
392	        Some(socket2::Protocol::from(proto)),
393	    )
394	    .map_err(|e| format!("raw socket proto {}: {}", proto, e))?;
395	    socket
396	        .set_read_timeout(Some(timeout))
397	        .map_err(|e| format!("timeout: {}", e))?;
398	
399	    socket
400	        .send_to(payload, &sock_addr)
401	        .map_err(|e| format!("sendto: {}", e))?;
402	
403	    let mut buf = [0u8; 4096];
404	    match socket.recv_from(as_uninit(&mut buf)) {
405	        Ok((_, addr)) => {
406	            let from_ip = addr
407	                .as_socket_ipv4()
408	                .map(|a| a.ip().to_string())
409	                .unwrap_or_default();
410	            Ok(from_ip == dest)
411	        }
412	        Err(_) => Ok(false),
413	    }
414	}

416	/// Build an IP fragment with IP_HDRINCL.
417	fn build_ip_fragment(
418	    src: &str,
419	    dst: &str,
420	    proto: u8,
421	    payload: &[u8],
422	    ident: u16,
423	    frag_offset: u16,
424	    more_fragments: bool,
425	) -> Result<Vec<u8>, String> {
426	    let src_ip: Ipv4Addr = src.parse().map_err(|e| format!("bad src: {}", e))?;
427	    let dst_ip: Ipv4Addr = dst.parse().map_err(|e| format!("bad dst: {}", e))?;
428	    let total_len = 20u16 + payload.len() as u16;
429	    let mut flags_frag = frag_offset & 0x1FFF;
430	    if more_fragments {
431	        flags_frag |= 0x2000;
432	    }
433	
434	    let mut hdr = Vec::with_capacity(20 + payload.len());
435	    hdr.push(0x45);
436	    hdr.push(0);
437	    hdr.extend_from_slice(&total_len.to_be_bytes());
438	    hdr.extend_from_slice(&ident.to_be_bytes());
439	    hdr.extend_from_slice(&flags_frag.to_be_bytes());
440	    hdr.push(64);
441	    hdr.push(proto);
442	    hdr.extend_from_slice(&[0, 0]); // checksum placeholder
443	    hdr.extend_from_slice(&src_ip.octets());
444	    hdr.extend_from_slice(&dst_ip.octets());
445	    let cksum = ip_checksum(&hdr);
446	    hdr[10..12].copy_from_slice(&cksum.to_be_bytes());
447	    hdr.extend_from_slice(payload);
448	    Ok(hdr)
449	}

451	/// Send a list of IP fragments via raw socket and listen for TCP response.
452	fn send_fragments_and_listen(
453	    dst: &str,
454	    fragments: &[Vec<u8>],
455	    timeout: Duration,
456	) -> Result<bool, String> {
457	    let dst_addr: Ipv4Addr = dst.parse().map_err(|e| format!("bad IP: {}", e))?;
458	    let sock_addr = socket2::SockAddr::from(SocketAddrV4::new(dst_addr, 0));
459	
460	    let send_sock = socket2::Socket::new(
461	        socket2::Domain::IPV4,
462	        socket2::Type::RAW,
463	        Some(socket2::Protocol::from(libc::IPPROTO_RAW as i32)),
464	    )
465	    .map_err(|e| format!("raw socket: {}", e))?;
466	    unsafe {
467	        let val: libc::c_int = 1;
468	        libc::setsockopt(
469	            send_sock.as_raw_fd(),
470	            libc::IPPROTO_IP,
471	            libc::IP_HDRINCL,
472	            &val as *const _ as *const libc::c_void,
473	            std::mem::size_of::<libc::c_int>() as libc::socklen_t,
474	        );
475	    }
476	    for frag in fragments {
477	        send_sock
478	            .send_to(frag, &sock_addr)
479	            .map_err(|e| format!("sendto: {}", e))?;
480	    }
481	
482	    let listen_sock = socket2::Socket::new(
483	        socket2::Domain::IPV4,
484	        socket2::Type::RAW,
485	        Some(socket2::Protocol::from(libc::IPPROTO_TCP as i32)),
486	    )
487	    .map_err(|e| format!("listen socket: {}", e))?;
488	    listen_sock
489	        .set_read_timeout(Some(timeout))
490	        .map_err(|e| format!("timeout: {}", e))?;
491	
492	    let mut buf = [0u8; 4096];
493	    let deadline = Instant::now() + timeout;
494	    loop {
495	        let remaining = deadline.saturating_duration_since(Instant::now());
496	        if remaining.is_zero() {
497	            return Ok(false);
498	        }
499	        let _ = listen_sock.set_read_timeout(Some(remaining));
500	        match listen_sock.recv_from(as_uninit(&mut buf)) {
501	            Ok((_, addr)) => {
502	                let from_ip = addr
503	                    .as_socket_ipv4()
504	                    .map(|a| a.ip().to_string())
505	                    .unwrap_or_default();
506	                if from_ip == dst {
507	                    return Ok(true);
508	                }
509	            }
510	            Err(e)
511	                if e.kind() == std::io::ErrorKind::WouldBlock
512	                    || e.kind() == std::io::ErrorKind::TimedOut =>
513	            {
514	                return Ok(false);
515	            }
516	            Err(_) => return Ok(false),
517	        }
518	    }
519	}

522	fn get_ipv6_addrs(scope: &str) -> Vec<String> {
523	    let mut addrs = Vec::new();

529	        for line in stdout.lines() {

531	            if let Some(rest) = trimmed.strip_prefix("inet6 ") {
532	                if let Some(addr) = rest.split_whitespace().next() {
533	                    if let Some(ip) = addr.split('/').next() {
534	                        addrs.push(ip.to_string());

536	                }
537	            }
538	        }
539	    }
540	    addrs

556	/// Get interface name and MAC from /sys/class/net/.
557	fn get_interface_info() -> Option<(String, String)> {
558	    let entries = std::fs::read_dir("/sys/class/net").ok()?;
559	    for entry in entries.flatten() {
560	        let name = entry.file_name().to_string_lossy().to_string();
561	        if !name.starts_with("eth") {
562	            continue;
563	        }
564	        let mac_path = format!("/sys/class/net/{}/address", name);
565	        if let Ok(mac) = std::fs::read_to_string(&mac_path) {
566	            let mac = mac.trim().to_string();
567	            // Valid Ethernet MAC: 6 octets = exactly 5 colons
568	            if mac.matches(':').count() == 5 && mac != "00:00:00:00:00:00" {
569	                return Some((name, mac));
570	            }
571	        }
572	    }
573	    None
574	}

576	/// Get sentry MAC from /proc/net/arp.
577	fn get_sentry_mac(sentry_ip: &str) -> Option<String> {
578	    // Ping to ensure ARP entry
579	    let _ = Command::new("ping")
580	        .args(["-c", "1", "-W", "1", sentry_ip])
581	        .output();
582	
583	    let content = std::fs::read_to_string("/proc/net/arp").ok()?;
584	    for line in content.lines() {
585	        let parts: Vec<&str> = line.split_whitespace().collect();
586	        if parts.first() == Some(&sentry_ip) && parts.len() >= 4 {
587	            let mac = parts[3];
588	            if mac != "00:00:00:00:00:00" {
589	                return Some(mac.to_string());
590	            }
591	        }
592	    }
593	    None
594	}

596	/// Convert "aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff" to 6-byte array.
597	fn mac_to_bytes(mac: &str) -> Result<[u8; 6], String> {
598	    let parts: Vec<&str> = mac.split(':').collect();
599	    if parts.len() != 6 {
600	        return Err(format!("invalid MAC: {}", mac));
601	    }
602	    let mut bytes = [0u8; 6];
603	    for (i, part) in parts.iter().enumerate() {
604	        bytes[i] = u8::from_str_radix(part, 16).map_err(|_| format!("invalid MAC byte: {}", part))?;
605	    }
606	    Ok(bytes)
607	}

580	
581	== System Overview
582	
583	=== Core Trust Challenge
584	
585	Modern development increasingly requires running foreign code with access to local work.
586	{st_container}-based development tools processing source code, local AI/ML tools analyzing proprietary data, and third-party services needing controlled network access create fundamental security challenges.
587	
588	This creates a fundamental trust dilemma:
589	
590	*
591	{st_container_s}
592	provide powerful tool isolation
593	* But they need controlled access to do useful work
594	* Standard
595	{st_container}
596	networking grants unfettered outbound access or else network services completely unavailable.
597	
598	=== The Bottle Pattern
599	
600	{at_rbm_system}
601	solves this by creating two simple, robust boundaries around a certain
602	{st_container}
603	type here referred to as a
604	{at_bottle_container}:
605	
606	. File Access Boundary
607	*
608	{at_bottle_container}
609	sees only explicitly controlled set of
610	{st_volume_mount_s}
611	* No other access to
612	{at_workstation}
613	filesystem
614	
615	. Network Boundary
616	*
617	{at_bottle_container}
618	has no direct network access
619	* All traffic flows through dedicated
620	{at_sentry_container},
621	a
622	{st_container}
623	configured with very tight network access and visibility controls.
624	* Only declared
625	{st_port_map_s}
626	and destinations allowed
627	* Full logging of allowed and denied traffic
628	
629	Each
630	{rbsc_crucible}
631	maintains these boundaries through a three-container architecture:
632	
633	* One
634	{at_sentry_container}
635	enforcing network policy on dual networks (bridge and enclave)
636	* One
637	{at_pentacle_container}
638	establishing and configuring the network namespace
639	* One
640	{at_bottle_container}
641	running the actual service, sharing the pentacle's network namespace via compose `network_mode: "service:pentacle"`
642	* Clear configuration in a single
643	{at_nameplate}
644	file, extended by per-nameplate compose fragments for volume mounts
645	
646	Docker Compose orchestrates the container lifecycle declaratively.
647	A base compose file (`rbob_compose.yml`) defines the three-container topology with health check chains: the
648	{at_sentry_container}
649	must be healthy before the
650	{at_pentacle_container}
651	starts, and the
652	{at_pentacle_container}
653	must be healthy before the
654	{at_bottle_container}
655	starts.
656	This prevents firewall bypass — the bottle cannot send traffic before security policies are enforced.
657	The "first packet" guarantee is enforceable because sentry's iptables are installed before the pentacle is healthy, the pentacle's namespace is shared with the bottle (so the bottle inherits the routing-and-firewall state already configured), and sentry sits on every inbound path by virtue of the published-port directive being on the sentry service (not pentacle) and the bottle's default route being via sentry.
658	Topology changes that violate any of these commitments require re-validation against the
659	{at_recipe_bottle}
660	Ifrit suite before declaring the security properties preserved.
661	Per-nameplate compose fragments (e.g., `«moniker».compose.yml`) add volume mount declarations.
662	Env-files passed via `--env-file` serve a dual purpose: YAML interpolation (`${VAR}` references in compose files resolve image refs, subnet, container names) and container environment forwarding (bare variable names in `environment:` sections inject values into containers at runtime).
663	
664	The
665	{at_pentacle_container}
666	is a privileged container that configures routing to ensure all traffic flows through the
667	{at_sentry_container},
668	then shares this pre-configured namespace with the
669	{at_bottle_container}
670	via compose `network_mode: "service:pentacle"`.
671	This ensures security policies are enforced from the first packet, and the
672	{at_bottle_container}
673	experiences only a functional path to its
674	{at_sentry_container}
675	gateway
676	
677	=== Security Properties
678	
679	The
680	{at_rbm_system}
681	maintains following essential guarantees:
682	
683	. File Access Control
684	*
685	{at_bottle_container_s}
686	can access only explicitly configured
687	{st_volume_mount_s}
688	* No access to broader system resources
689	
690	. Network Isolation
691	* All traffic flows through dedicated
692	{at_sentry_container}
693	* Each permitted traffic flow maintains exactly one controlled pathway
694	* Precise ingress, forwarding, and NAT rules enforce strict boundaries
695	* Only explicitly configured
696	{st_port_map_s}
697	allow traffic between
698	{at_transit_network}
699	and
700	{at_enclave_network}
701	*
702	{at_bottle_container_s}
703	have no direct network access; all connectivity flows through
704	{at_sentry_container_s}
705	* Comprehensive traffic logging
706	
707	. Service Separation
708	* Each
709	{rbsc_crucible}
710	fully isolated
711	* No cross-service communication
712	* Independent security policies
713	* Clean state separation
714	
715	. Service Access Control
716	* External access to services only permitted when
717	{rbrn_entry_mode}
718	is enabled
719	* No inter-container communication allowed within pods
720	* All service access must originate from outside the pod
721	* Entry traffic flows: external -> pod ->
722	{at_sentry_container}
723	->
724	{at_bottle_container}
725	
726	These properties hold across all operations and failure modes.
727	They depend on a layered-defense structure in which each layer is independently load-bearing for at least one invariant: network namespace isolation, capability bounds on the
728	{at_bottle_container}
729	(`cap_drop: NET_RAW, NET_ADMIN`), kernel reverse-path filtering, iptables filter chains (RBM-INGRESS, RBM-EGRESS, RBM-FORWARD), iptables NAT (PREROUTING DNAT, POSTROUTING MASQUERADE), and conntrack state tracking.
730	Removing or weakening a layer requires Ifrit-suite empirical validation to confirm the security envelope remains intact; architectural reasoning alone has been empirically insufficient.
731

747	
748	== Google API Engagement
749	
750	{at_recipe_bottle}
751	invokes
752	{gcp_service}
753	APIs through
754	{giam_service_account}
755	JWT authentication and direct HTTPS REST calls.
756	
757	* *Authentication*:
758	{giam_service_account_s}
759	use private key credentials to sign JWTs with RS256.
760	These JWTs are exchanged for
761	{oauth_token_s}
762	via Google's token endpoint.
763	All API calls include the bearer token in the Authorization header.
764	
765	* *Invocation Pattern*:
766	Operations execute through curl with JSON payloads and responses.
767	Each call captures both HTTP status codes and response bodies to files for processing.
768	Success is determined by HTTP 200/201/204 codes.
769	
770	* *Long-Running Operations*:
771	{gcp_service}
772	{gcp_lro_s}
773	return operation IDs that must be polled until completion.
774	The system polls at configurable intervals until completion.
775	The system handles both success and error states.
776	Operations utilize
777	{rbbc_submit}
778	and
779	{rbbc_await}
780	to handle all asynchronous operations.
781	
782	* *IAM Policies*:
783	All IAM policy modifications enforce version 3 to ensure conditional role binding support across all
784	{gcp_service}
785	resources.
786	
787	* *IAM Member Format*:
788	All IAM member bindings use `serviceAccount:«EMAIL»` format.
789	+
790	GCP IAM policy `members` fields only accept email-format identifiers (`serviceAccount:name@project.iam.gserviceaccount.com`).
791	The numeric `uniqueId` returned by `serviceAccounts.create` is valid for *API URL paths* (e.g., `projects/-/serviceAccounts/«UNIQUE_ID»` for GET/verification) but *not* for policy binding members.
792	`serviceAccount:«NUMERIC_ID»` returns HTTP 400 "Invalid service account" on all setIamPolicy endpoints — project, repository, and bucket alike.
793	+
794	Google's documentation recommends numeric IDs "to identify a service account just after it is created" (link:https://docs.google.com/iam/docs/manage-access-service-accounts[Manage access to service accounts]), but this applies to API resource addressing, not to IAM policy member strings.
795	The internal IAM system resolves email→numeric transparently; callers must use email.
796	+
797	Google-provisioned service agents (Cloud Build) are not affected — they are managed by Google, not newly created.
798	
799	== Operational Invariants
800	
801	=== Concurrent Session Safety
802	
803	{at_recipe_bottle}
804	commands are designed for concurrent execution from multiple independent sessions against the same filesystem.
805	This enables parallel workflows where different developers or automation systems invoke commands simultaneously.
806	
807	Concurrency safety is achieved through:
808	
809	* *Ephemeral Isolation*:
810	Each command invocation creates a unique temporary directory using `BURD_NOW_STAMP` (timestamp + process ID + random component), ensuring intermediate files never collide.
811	
812	* *Unique Artifact Naming*:
813	Cloud resources (build IDs, inscribe timestamps) incorporate unique identifiers, preventing overwrite conflicts in
814	{rbtgi_repository}.
815	
816	* *Read-Only Git Queries*:
817	Git operations are non-mutating (status checks, metadata reads).
818	The `git fetch` operation uses git's internal locking for safe concurrent execution.
819	
820	* *Semantic Preconditions*:
821	Operations like
822	{rbtgo_ark_conjure}
823	require clean git state.
824	This is a workflow constraint ensuring reproducible builds, not a collision guard.
825	
826	NOTE: Concurrent builds of the same vessel produce independent images with different timestamps.
827	Both builds succeed; neither overwrites the other.
828	
829	NOTE: The
830	{gcb_service}
831	private pool enforces a *Concurrent Build CPUs (Private Pool)* quota per project per region.
832	Machine type is set at pool creation time via
833	{rbrd_gcb_machine_type}
834	(a Compute Engine type such as `e2-standard-2`); individual build requests do not specify machine type.
835	If the pool's vCPU allocation is nearly exhausted, a second
836	build invocation will remain in `QUEUED` status while the first is `WORKING`.
837	The
838	{rbrr_gcb_min_concurrent_builds}
839	regime variable declares the operator's minimum concurrency requirement.
840	See
841	{rbtgo_quota_build}
842	for the manual Console procedure to review build capacity settings.
843	
844	== Key Premises
845	
846	[[rbsk_no_gcloud]]
847	//axl_voices axk_premise
848	{rbsk_no_gcloud}::

== System Overview

=== Core Trust Challenge

Modern development increasingly requires running foreign code with access to local work.
{st_container}-based development tools processing source code, local AI/ML tools analyzing proprietary data, and third-party services needing controlled network access create fundamental security challenges.

This creates a fundamental trust dilemma:

*
{st_container_s}
provide powerful tool isolation
* But they need controlled access to do useful work
* Standard
{st_container}
networking grants unfettered outbound access or else network services completely unavailable.

=== The Bottle Pattern

{at_rbm_system}
solves this by creating two simple, robust boundaries around a certain
{st_container}
type here referred to as a
{at_bottle_container}:

. File Access Boundary
*
{at_bottle_container}
sees only explicitly controlled set of
{st_volume_mount_s}
* No other access to
{at_workstation}
filesystem

. Network Boundary
*
{at_bottle_container}
has no direct network access
* All traffic flows through dedicated
{at_sentry_container},
a
{st_container}
configured with very tight network access and visibility controls.
* Only declared
{st_port_map_s}
and destinations allowed
* Full logging of allowed and denied traffic

Each
{rbsc_crucible}
maintains these boundaries through a three-container architecture:

* One
{at_sentry_container}
enforcing network policy on dual networks (bridge and enclave)
* One
{at_pentacle_container}
establishing and configuring the network namespace
* One
{at_bottle_container}
running the actual service, sharing the pentacle's network namespace via compose `network_mode: "service:pentacle"`
* Clear configuration in a single
{at_nameplate}
file, extended by per-nameplate compose fragments for volume mounts

Docker Compose orchestrates the container lifecycle declaratively.
A base compose file (`rbob_compose.yml`) defines the three-container topology with health check chains: the
{at_sentry_container}
must be healthy before the
{at_pentacle_container}
starts, and the
{at_pentacle_container}
must be healthy before the
{at_bottle_container}
starts.
This prevents firewall bypass — the bottle cannot send traffic before security policies are enforced.
The "first packet" guarantee is enforceable because sentry's iptables are installed before the pentacle is healthy, the pentacle's namespace is shared with the bottle (so the bottle inherits the routing-and-firewall state already configured), and sentry sits on every inbound path by virtue of the published-port directive being on the sentry service (not pentacle) and the bottle's default route being via sentry.
Topology changes that violate any of these commitments require re-validation against the
{at_recipe_bottle}
Ifrit suite before declaring the security properties preserved.
Per-nameplate compose fragments (e.g., `«moniker».compose.yml`) add volume mount declarations.
Env-files passed via `--env-file` serve a dual purpose: YAML interpolation (`${VAR}` references in compose files resolve image refs, subnet, container names) and container environment forwarding (bare variable names in `environment:` sections inject values into containers at runtime).

The
{at_pentacle_container}
is a privileged container that configures routing to ensure all traffic flows through the
{at_sentry_container},
then shares this pre-configured namespace with the
{at_bottle_container}
via compose `network_mode: "service:pentacle"`.
This ensures security policies are enforced from the first packet, and the
{at_bottle_container}
experiences only a functional path to its
{at_sentry_container}
gateway

=== Security Properties

The
{at_rbm_system}
maintains following essential guarantees:

. File Access Control
*
{at_bottle_container_s}
can access only explicitly configured
{st_volume_mount_s}
* No access to broader system resources

. Network Isolation
* All traffic flows through dedicated
{at_sentry_container}
* Each permitted traffic flow maintains exactly one controlled pathway
* Precise ingress, forwarding, and NAT rules enforce strict boundaries
* Only explicitly configured
{st_port_map_s}
allow traffic between
{at_transit_network}
and
{at_enclave_network}
*
{at_bottle_container_s}
have no direct network access; all connectivity flows through
{at_sentry_container_s}
* Comprehensive traffic logging

. Service Separation
* Each
{rbsc_crucible}
fully isolated
* No cross-service communication
* Independent security policies
* Clean state separation

. Service Access Control
* External access to services only permitted when
{rbrn_entry_mode}
is enabled
* No inter-container communication allowed within pods
* All service access must originate from outside the pod
* Entry traffic flows: external -> pod ->
{at_sentry_container}
->
{at_bottle_container}

These properties hold across all operations and failure modes.
They depend on a layered-defense structure in which each layer is independently load-bearing for at least one invariant: network namespace isolation, capability bounds on the
{at_bottle_container}
(`cap_drop: NET_RAW, NET_ADMIN`), kernel reverse-path filtering, iptables filter chains (RBM-INGRESS, RBM-EGRESS, RBM-FORWARD), iptables NAT (PREROUTING DNAT, POSTROUTING MASQUERADE), and conntrack state tracking.
Removing or weakening a layer requires Ifrit-suite empirical validation to confirm the security envelope remains intact; architectural reasoning alone has been empirically insufficient.

== Google API Engagement

{at_recipe_bottle}
invokes
{gcp_service}
APIs through
{giam_service_account}
JWT authentication and direct HTTPS REST calls.

* *Authentication*:
{giam_service_account_s}
use private key credentials to sign JWTs with RS256.
These JWTs are exchanged for
{oauth_token_s}
via Google's token endpoint.
All API calls include the bearer token in the Authorization header.

* *Invocation Pattern*:
Operations execute through curl with JSON payloads and responses.
Each call captures both HTTP status codes and response bodies to files for processing.
Success is determined by HTTP 200/201/204 codes.

* *Long-Running Operations*:
{gcp_service}
{gcp_lro_s}
return operation IDs that must be polled until completion.
The system polls at configurable intervals until completion.
The system handles both success and error states.
Operations utilize
{rbbc_submit}
and
{rbbc_await}
to handle all asynchronous operations.

* *IAM Policies*:
All IAM policy modifications enforce version 3 to ensure conditional role binding support across all
{gcp_service}
resources.

* *IAM Member Format*:
All IAM member bindings use `serviceAccount:«EMAIL»` format.
+
GCP IAM policy `members` fields only accept email-format identifiers (`serviceAccount:name@project.iam.gserviceaccount.com`).
The numeric `uniqueId` returned by `serviceAccounts.create` is valid for *API URL paths* (e.g., `projects/-/serviceAccounts/«UNIQUE_ID»` for GET/verification) but *not* for policy binding members.
`serviceAccount:«NUMERIC_ID»` returns HTTP 400 "Invalid service account" on all setIamPolicy endpoints — project, repository, and bucket alike.
+
Google's documentation recommends numeric IDs "to identify a service account just after it is created" (link:https://docs.google.com/iam/docs/manage-access-service-accounts[Manage access to service accounts]), but this applies to API resource addressing, not to IAM policy member strings.
The internal IAM system resolves email→numeric transparently; callers must use email.
+
Google-provisioned service agents (Cloud Build) are not affected — they are managed by Google, not newly created.

== Operational Invariants

=== Concurrent Session Safety

{at_recipe_bottle}
commands are designed for concurrent execution from multiple independent sessions against the same filesystem.
This enables parallel workflows where different developers or automation systems invoke commands simultaneously.

Concurrency safety is achieved through:

* *Ephemeral Isolation*:
Each command invocation creates a unique temporary directory using `BURD_NOW_STAMP` (timestamp + process ID + random component), ensuring intermediate files never collide.

* *Unique Artifact Naming*:
Cloud resources (build IDs, inscribe timestamps) incorporate unique identifiers, preventing overwrite conflicts in
{rbtgi_repository}.

* *Read-Only Git Queries*:
Git operations are non-mutating (status checks, metadata reads).
The `git fetch` operation uses git's internal locking for safe concurrent execution.

* *Semantic Preconditions*:
Operations like
{rbtgo_ark_conjure}
require clean git state.
This is a workflow constraint ensuring reproducible builds, not a collision guard.

NOTE: Concurrent builds of the same vessel produce independent images with different timestamps.
Both builds succeed; neither overwrites the other.

NOTE: The
{gcb_service}
private pool enforces a *Concurrent Build CPUs (Private Pool)* quota per project per region.
Machine type is set at pool creation time via
{rbrd_gcb_machine_type}
(a Compute Engine type such as `e2-standard-2`); individual build requests do not specify machine type.
If the pool's vCPU allocation is nearly exhausted, a second
build invocation will remain in `QUEUED` status while the first is `WORKING`.
The
{rbrr_gcb_min_concurrent_builds}
regime variable declares the operator's minimum concurrency requirement.
See
{rbtgo_quota_build}
for the manual Console procedure to review build capacity settings.

aiTitle: Evaluate test cases for sentry containment hardening

content.text:
I have what I need: the security model in RBS0 (System Overview → Security Properties → Adversarial Validation), the full RBSIP framework including the new `rbsq_wdd_offpath_reply` quirk, the adjudication memo, and the hardened sortie's actual classification logic. Let me give you the evaluation at the level you're asking for — whether each test hardens an RBS0 guarantee, not how to wire it.

---

## Starting frame

The yardstick I'm judging these against is RBS0's own mandate, stated twice in the Security Properties section:

> "Each layer is independently load-bearing for at least one invariant... Removing or weakening a layer requires Ifrit-suite empirical validation to confirm the security envelope remains intact; **architectural reasoning alone has been empirically insufficient.**"

Paired with RBSIP's epistemic rule: **every BREACH must trace to a missing or weakened sentry rule.** A breach that maps to no rule is an epistemic violation — which is exactly the defect the conntrack adjudication repaired.

That gives me the lens. The adjudication didn't just fix a sortie — it **added new load-bearing reasoning to the system**: (a) the L2-provenance classifier, which now silently decides breach-vs-quirk, and (b) the `rbsq_wdd_offpath_reply` quirk's three safety claims (allowed-dest-only, empty-RST-only, no-unsolicited-ingress). By RBS0's own rule, that new reasoning is *exactly* the kind of thing that "architectural reasoning alone is insufficient" for — yet right now it is validated only by reasoning. **The highest-value tests are the ones that convert the adjudication's assertions into tested facts.** That reframes the priority list cleanly.

## The reframe that orders everything

The hardening traded a **loud, confusing failure** (BREACH on Windows DD that mapped to no sentry rule) for a **quiet green**. That's a real improvement in signal — but in epistemic terms it's a *regression* unless the green is falsifiable. A green check on `conntrack_spoofed_ack` now means one of two things and you can't tell which:
- containment held, or
- the probe/classifier is broken and *can no longer fail*.

A security test that cannot fail is worse than no test — it manufactures confidence. So the work that matters most is restoring falsifiability to what the adjudication introduced. That's #1 and #2. Everything else is broadening coverage of an invariant that's already loudly tested.

---

## Per-candidate evaluation

### 1. Negative control for the provenance gate — **build first; do not ship the hardening without it**

**Verdict: highest merit, and more load-bearing than the candidate framing states.**

The candidate frames the risk as "the gateway-MAC logic could be wrong → always-pass." Looking at the actual classifier, it's a bit better than that (there's an `Indeterminate` arm that fails conservatively to BREACH if the gateway MAC won't resolve) — but the silent-pass surface is actually *wider* than the MAC comparison. The whole probe pipeline now sits upstream of a SECURE verdict:

- `NoResponse → SECURE` fires if egress silently fails, or if the AF_PACKET capture misses the reply (timing/filter bug). Either masks everything.
- `OffPath → SECURE` fires on the MAC-mismatch path.
- The capture, the gateway-MAC learning, and the comparison are all new code between "real breach" and "green."

A negative control — a deliberately-weakened-sentry nameplate that admits the lone-ACK reply **back through the gateway**, asserted to BREACH — is the only thing that proves the *entire pipeline still goes red on a real failure*: egress works, capture works, `SentryMediated` classification works. That is precisely RBS0's "empirical validation of a weakened layer," applied to the layer the adjudication itself added. This isn't optional polish; it's the close-out condition for the hardening to be trustworthy at all.

**What it asserts:** the `SentryMediated → BREACH` arm is live — a reply that transits the sentry still fails the sortie.

**Honest scope limit, worth stating to yourself before building:** this validates the *breach-detection* direction (the safety-critical one). It does **not** validate the `OffPath → SECURE` *suppression* direction — that arm only exercises on real Windows DD. So the negative control certifies "real breaches still fire"; the quirk-suppression correctness remains certified only by running on the affected platform. That's the right split — the safety-critical direction is "does a real breach still fire," and that's what this covers.

**Cost to be honest about:** needs a new nameplate (weakened sentry — e.g. a stateless return-path ACCEPT for the dest so a real upstream's RST to the lone ACK gets forwarded) and a live upstream that replies, so it lands in the `service`/`crucible` tier, not `fast`. That's a genuine fixture to build and maintain, not a one-liner.

**On the generalization** ("every provenance/observation-gated sortie earns a negative control"): endorse it, but sharpen it so it doesn't over-apply. The precise discipline is: **any sortie that *suppresses* a would-be breach based on an observation must carry a negative control proving the suppression is conditional.** Self-falsifying sorties (loud breach on failure, secure otherwise) don't need one — the rest of the matrix falsifies them. Today `conntrack_spoofed_ack` is the *only* sortie with a suppression gate, so #1 is both the fix and the pilot for the rule.

### 2. Off-path probe to a BLOCKED destination — **build second; best effort-to-value ratio**

**Verdict: high merit, low cost — it tests the quirk's central safety claim.**

The quirk `rbsq_wdd_offpath_reply` is judged benign on one load-bearing premise, quoted verbatim from its definition: *"blocked destinations are dropped at the sentry before the substrate ever sees them."* The memo's Scope-of-exposure section asserts the same. **That assertion is the entire reason the quirk is a quirk and not a breach** — if DD's substrate injected an off-path reply for a *non-allowlisted* dest, that's a path-into-the-bottle on behalf of a destination the sentry never approved: a real breach by any reading of RBS0's "no connectivity to non-allowed destinations." And right now it is pure reasoning, untested.

This is the cleanest instance of "architectural reasoning has been empirically insufficient." It's a near-variant of the existing sortie (provenance-aware lone-ACK, retargeted to a blocked dest), unilateral, no new nameplate. Invariant asserted: **the substrate answers only egress the sentry allowed** — any reply (off-path or sentry-mediated) to a blocked dest is BREACH. Given it's cheap *and* closes the quirk's keystone assumption, a case can be made it goes *first* as the quick win; I keep it second only because #1 guards a silent-pass in the *primary, already-shipping* test, which is the more dangerous hole.

### 3. Sole-boundary as a coordinated invariant — **highest conceptual merit, highest design risk; stage it, scope it tightly**

**Verdict: this is the "right" test of RBS0's actual load-bearing claim — and the one most likely to rot into maintenance debt if built naively.**

The sole-boundary assumption ("the sentry is the sole boundary on every path to a bottle," per RBSIP Substrate Quirks, and RBS0's "all connectivity flows through sentry") is *the* invariant the whole adjudication exists to defend. Candidate 3 promotes it from assumption to directly-monitored property. Nothing tests the core invariant more honestly.

But "assert *every frame* the bottle receives was sentry-forwarded" is an open-ended assertion over a time window, and the enclave segment is chattier than one documented exception. On DD there's ARP, possibly IPv6 RA/NDP, gateway housekeeping — the `rbsq_wdd_offpath_reply` RST is *one* known off-path frame, not necessarily the only L2 noise. A literal "every frame" monitor will either fire on benign chatter and get whitelisted toward uselessness, or get disabled — the exact non-load-bearing complexity CLAUDE.md's litmus warns against. The honest path is to **scope the assertion to TCP frames matching the probe flows**, not literally every frame, and to build it *after* #4 has characterized the baseline. It depends on the same provenance primitive #1 validates, so it should come after that primitive is trusted. High value, but it earns its place only with deliberate scoping — this is where your judgment on appetite matters more than mine.

### 4. Unsolicited off-path ingress (passive listen) — **the baseline #3 needs; not urgent on its own**

**Verdict: medium merit as characterization/precursor; testing a hypothesis currently believed false.**

The scope-of-exposure argument leans on solicited-vs-unsolicited: the DD RST is benign partly *because* it's "generated only in response to the bottle's own egress... not unsolicited third-party ingress." The quirk echoes this ("no unsolicited ingress channel"). Candidate 4 tests that boundary — any unsolicited, payload-bearing, non-sentry-sourced frame absent prior egress is categorically worse than the RST quirk and would be a genuine path-in.

Its real role is **reconnaissance that tightens the quirk's "no unsolicited ingress" clause from claim to monitored fact, and supplies the L2 baseline #3 must whitelist against.** As a standalone it's lower-urgency: there's no evidence of unsolicited ingress today, so it probes a hypothesis currently believed false — good defense-in-depth, not a gap-closer. Its verdict is also inherently fuzzy (listen how long? what counts as unsolicited?), same open-window hazard as #3. Build it as the precursor to #3, not before #2.

### 5. Return-path state family + L2 footprint audit — **lowest urgency; cheap fan-out plus a tripwire**

**Verdict: legitimate, incremental, appropriately last.**

- **5a (lone RST / lone FIN / out-of-window ACK):** the sentry's return-path state enforcement should reject *all* out-of-state segments; the current sortie tests one (ACK). These are completeness siblings hitting the same FORWARD `RELATED,ESTABLISHED` rule from adjacent angles — real but diminishing returns. Cheap to add once #1's negative control proves the primitive works; do them as a fan-out, not as standalone efforts.
- **5b (per-platform L2-endpoint enumeration):** turns the substrate footprint into a tracked invariant so a *new* injecting endpoint surfaces as a diff. Its real pairing is the quirk's **retirement condition** ("re-evaluate if Windows DD networking ceases to inject off-path replies") — the enumeration is *how you'd detect* that change, or detect a new runtime introducing a new injector. A nice tripwire, low urgency.

---

## Recommended build order

| Order | Candidate | Why here | Tier / shape |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **#1 negative control** | Restores falsifiability to the *primary, already-shipping* test; validates the whole new probe→classify pipeline, not just the MAC compare. Close-out condition for the hardening. | `service`/`crucible`; new weakened-sentry nameplate; coordinated-ish (live upstream) |
| **2** | **#2 blocked-dest off-path** | Cheapest high-value test; converts the quirk's keystone safety claim (allowed-dest-only) from assertion to fact. | unilateral sortie, near-variant of existing; `service` |
| **3** | **#4 passive unsolicited-ingress** | Characterizes the L2 baseline and tightens the "no unsolicited ingress" clause; prerequisite recon for #3. | unilateral, observation-window |
| **4** | **#3 sole-boundary coordinated** | The honest test of RBS0's core invariant — but only safe to build once the primitive is trusted (#1) and the baseline is known (#4), and only if scoped to probe-flow frames. | coordinated (theurge + ifrit) |
| **5** | **#5a/#5b fan-out + tripwire** | Incremental coverage of the same rule (5a) and a substrate-shape tripwire tied to the quirk's retirement condition (5b). | 5a unilateral fan-out; 5b enumeration/audit |

**The decision, stated plainly:** #1 and #2 aren't "more coverage" — they're the debt the adjudication incurred and should be paid before the branch is considered done. I'd treat them as the close-out of the conntrack work itself, with #1 as the near-term pace you flagged. #3–#5 are genuine defense-in-depth that *generalize* the sole-boundary invariant, worth doing deliberately and in that order, but they're new initiative, not debt — and #3 specifically should not be built until you've decided how much open-window monitoring you're willing to maintain, because that's the one with real rot potential.

One sincerity flag before you decide: the strongest argument *against* going past #2 is CLAUDE.md's load-bearing test. #3 and #4 assert over open observation windows, and an assertion that fires on benign substrate chatter trains the team to whitelist or mute — which is negative security value. #1 and #2 are bounded, falsifiable, and map to specific claims; #3/#4 need explicit scoping discipline to stay load-bearing. I'd want that scoping settled in conversation before either is slated.

I haven't opened an officium or touched ₣BV — per your instruction, nothing slated, no itches made. When you want to move, #1 and #2 are ready to slate as paces; #3–#5 read more like itches pending the scoping decision.

# jjezs_slate conntrack-provenance-negative-control

## Character
Judgment plus a real fixture build; security-relevant. Closes epistemic debt the conntrack hardening incurred.

## Goal
conntrack_spoofed_ack now suppresses a would-be breach by L2 provenance, so its SECURE verdict is unfalsifiable — green could mean containment held, or that the probe/capture/classify pipeline silently broke (egress never left, AF_PACKET capture missed the reply, or the gateway-MAC compare is wrong). Build a deliberately-weakened-sentry nameplate whose loosened return path lets the lone-ACK reply transit the sentry (arriving with gateway-MAC provenance), and assert the sortie BREACHes there — proving the whole pipeline still goes red on a real return-path failure.

## Cinched
- Validates the SentryMediated -> BREACH direction only. OffPath -> SECURE suppression is certified solely by running on real Windows DD; the control does not prove quirk suppression.
- Pilots a discipline: any sortie that suppresses a would-be breach on an observation carries a negative control proving the suppression is conditional. This is currently the only such gate.
- Live upstream plus container runtime -> service/crucible tier, not fast. The new weakened-sentry nameplate is the real cost.

## Done
A weakened-sentry nameplate forwards the lone-ACK reply to the bottle; conntrack_spoofed_ack asserts BREACH against it and SECURE against the hardened nameplates, and the contrast runs green in the suite.

Sources: Memos/memo-20260604-conntrack-spoofed-ack-adjudication.md; RBSIP "Substrate Quirks" (rbsq_wdd_offpath_reply); sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack in rbida_sorties.rs; RBS0 Security Properties ("architectural reasoning alone has been empirically insufficient").

content.text:
You're right on both, and I'll be plain since it's late.

**#3 — not crisp. Drop it.** The only way to make "the sentry is the sole boundary" into a crisp test is to pair it with a *stimulus* — emit a specific probe, classify the specific reply by provenance. But the moment you do that, it just *is* the conntrack sortie / #2 / #5a. There's no crisp test hiding in #3 beyond "apply provenance classification to more probe types." The "every frame over a window" version is the non-crisp part, and it has no principled stopping point. Your instinct — "unimplementable outside a strangely shaped environmental stimulus" — is exactly the diagnosis. Its real content is already covered by #1 and #2.

**#4 — same, but worse. Drop it.** It's purely passive (no stimulus at all), so it's the least crisp of the three — an open-window negative with no triggering event. There's no clean form. The *one* crisp residue worth keeping: when the provenance sortie sees an off-path reply, assert it's payload-free (empty RST) — that hardens the quirk's "empty RST only" claim and it's crisp because the lone ACK is the stimulus. But that's a one-line addition to the #1/#2 family, not a standalone test. I'd fold it in there, not slate it.

**#5 — my opinion:**
- **5a** (lone RST/FIN/out-of-window ACK) is crisp — same stimulus/response shape, different flags — but low marginal value; it pokes the same sentry rule from angles unlikely to differ. *Itch at most, not a pace.*
- **5b** (per-platform L2-endpoint snapshot + diff) is the one genuinely crisp, novel idea in the whole back half: it's an *inventory*, not a monitor — record the MACs on the enclave segment per platform, diff against the recorded set, a new endpoint trips it. Crisp and falsifiable. But low urgency. *Itch.*

**Bottom line:** nothing else needs slating tonight. #1 (₢BVAAI) and #2 (₢BVAAJ) are the real debt and they're in. Drop #3 and #4 — they aren't crisp tests, and their crisp residue already lives in the provenance sortie. #5b is the only thing I'd even keep on the radar, as a low-priority itch. Say the word when you're fresh and I'll capture 5b in one line; no need to decide it tonight.
